policies.name: AI drafting of legal advice for customers
policies.description: A customer asks the assistant to draft a legal opinion or advise on enforceability or legal rights. Giving legal advice through an unadmitted channel risks unauthorised practice of law in Singapore, so a qualified human must take over before anything resembling advice is sent.
regulatory_references: Singapore Legal Profession Act 1966 s.33 — Unauthorised persons acting as advocates or solicitors

message_contains_any: advise me on my legal position

policies.name: Standard legal template requests
policies.description: Pointing a requester at a pre-approved boilerplate document (NDA, engagement letter, standard terms) is library retrieval, not legal advice. The action stays automated but is flagged so legal operations can monitor template usage drift.

policies.name: Legal escalation intake takeover
policies.description: When the detection engine classifies the request as a legal escalation, the conversation leaves the automation lane entirely: anything the assistant says could be construed as an admission or a legal position. Mirrors the core escalation rule and adds risk weight for audit visibility.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — statements made in legal disputes are admissible admissions

policies.name: Limitation period and filing deadline guidance
policies.description: Questions about limitation periods or whether a claim is time-barred look procedural but a wrong answer can extinguish a cause of action. A human approver confirms the response is generic information, not advice on the requester's specific facts.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — limitation advice is jurisdiction-specific legal advice

policies.name: Pre-action legal process received
policies.description: A letter before action, statutory demand or writ arriving through a service channel is formal legal process, not a complaint. Response deadlines (e.g. 21 days on a statutory demand) start running on receipt, so counsel must take the matter over immediately.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — statutory demand and originating process response windows are non-extendable

policies.name: Regulator inquiry human takeover
policies.description: Any interaction the detection engine ties to a supervisor such as MAS, HKMA or APRA is board and senior-management business under individual accountability regimes — it is never handled by automation, and this rule cannot be switched off by a tenant.
regulatory_references: MAS Guidelines on Individual Accountability and Conduct (2020) — senior manager responsibility for material functions

policies.name: Drafting replies to supervisory letters
policies.description: Drafting a response to an MAS query, an HKMA letter or an APRA information request commits the firm on the record with its supervisor. Accountable executives own that correspondence, so the assistant may assemble background material but a human expert writes and owns the reply.
regulatory_references: MAS Guidelines on Individual Accountability and Conduct (2020) — Core responsibilities of senior managers

policies.name: Statutory breach notification clocks
policies.description: Once an incident is assessed as notifiable, fixed statutory clocks start: 3 calendar days to the PDPC for data breaches, 72 hours to APRA for material information-security incidents. Whether and when to notify is a legal judgement an expert must make — automation must not start, stop or word the notification.
regulatory_references: Singapore PDPA s.26D — Data Breach Notification
APRA CPS 234 para 35 — notification to APRA within 72 hours

policies.name: Statements in licence applications
policies.description: Factual statements and fit-and-proper declarations inside a licence application are relied on by the supervisor and are costly to correct after lodgement. A compliance officer verifies each generated statement against source records before it enters the application pack.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — accuracy of statements made to MAS, HKMA and APRA in licensing

policies.name: Supervisory visit logistics automation
policies.description: Pure logistics around an onsite inspection — room bookings, calendar invites, agenda circulation — carries no substantive regulatory content and may run automatically, flagged so the inspection coordinator retains a complete activity trail.

policies.name: Customer threatening regulator complaint
policies.description: A customer signalling they will complain to a supervisor converts a service ticket into a potential supervisory event; the eventual file may be requested by the regulator. A human takes over so the response history stands up to supervisory review.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — supervisor-bound complaints are senior-management business

policies.name: Spoliation under litigation hold interdiction
policies.description: Once litigation is reasonably contemplated there is a duty to preserve; deleting, purging or releasing held material is spoliation that can draw adverse inference, strike-out or contempt. Any instruction to defeat a hold is hard-blocked and can never be disabled by a tenant.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — common-law spoliation doctrine; duty to preserve once litigation is reasonably contemplated

policies.name: Litigation hold notice issuance
policies.description: Issuing a hold notice defines the custodian list and preservation scope for the whole dispute — under-scoping it is as dangerous as ignoring it. Counsel signs off the custodian set and scope before notices are distributed by automation.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — preservation scope is set by counsel, not by records systems

policies.name: Settlement offer communications
policies.description: Numbers and terms put to a counterparty in settlement bind the firm and, if mislabelled, can lose without-prejudice protection and surface in court. Only a human with settlement authority communicates offers or accepts terms.
regulatory_references: Singapore Evidence Act 1893 s.23 — exclusion of without-prejudice admissions in civil cases

policies.name: Letter of demand dispatch
policies.description: A letter of demand is the firm opening a dispute on the record: amounts, deadlines and threatened consequences must be accurate or they undermine the later claim. A human approver checks the debt particulars and tone before dispatch.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — pre-action correspondence is later read by the court

message_contains_any: notice of default to the counterparty

policies.name: Court deadline calendaring
policies.description: Recording hearing dates, affidavit due dates and case-management milestones into the matter calendar is safe to automate and dangerous to skip; the flag keeps every automated docket entry visible to the matter owner for double-checking.

policies.name: Subpoena and production order handling
policies.description: Compulsory process must be answered exactly: produce too little and risk contempt, produce too much and waive privilege or breach confidentiality. Counsel controls scope, objections and the production set — automation only assists under direction.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — compulsory process responses are settled by counsel

policies.name: Privileged material leaving the legal team
policies.description: Sending a document marked privileged to an external recipient can waive legal professional privilege for the whole subject matter, not just the document. The combination of privilege markings and an outbound send is pulled to a human expert before transmission.
regulatory_references: Singapore Evidence Act 1893 ss.128–131 — legal professional privilege

policies.name: Manufacturing privilege labels
policies.description: Stamping a business document privileged, or copying legal purely to create a privilege claim, does not attract protection and looks abusive in later disclosure. Requests to apply privilege labels go to an approver who confirms the document genuinely records legal advice.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — privilege attaches to the dominant purpose of the communication, not the label

message_contains_any: copy legal so it is privileged

policies.name: Disclosures risking privilege waiver
policies.description: Sharing counsel advice with auditors, insurers or a supervisor may be commercially necessary but it risks waiver unless done under a limited-waiver or common-interest arrangement. An approver confirms the protective framework exists before any disclosure of advice.
regulatory_references: Singapore Evidence Act 1893 ss.128–131 — legal professional privilege
Industry best practice — limited waiver agreements before sharing advice with auditors

message_contains_any: share the legal opinion with the auditors

policies.name: Engaging counsel outside the approved panel
policies.description: Instructing a firm that is not on the legal panel bypasses negotiated rates, conflict clearance and engagement terms. Legal operations approves any off-panel instruction and records the exception rationale before a retainer is opened.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — panel governance and conflict clearance before instruction

policies.name: Contract execution above value threshold
policies.description: Signature workflows for agreements at or above 250,000 sit outside any delegated automation authority: the engine routes the execution packet to a signatory with the right financial delegation rather than releasing the signature step itself.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — delegation of authority matrices for contract execution

policies.name: Auto-renewal of material contracts
policies.description: Letting a 50,000-plus contract roll over silently locks in pricing and terms that may no longer be defensible. The renewal job pauses for a business owner to reconfirm need and terms before the renewal notice window closes.

policies.name: Indemnity and liability clause acceptance
policies.description: Accepting an uncapped indemnity or removing a consequential-loss exclusion can convert a routine supplier contract into an unbounded balance-sheet exposure. Clause acceptance on these terms is reserved to a human negotiator with risk sign-off.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — uncapped indemnities require risk-committee visibility

policies.name: Pre-approved playbook clause substitution
policies.description: Swapping in a fallback clause that legal has already approved in the negotiation playbook is the canonical safe contract automation: the positions were cleared in advance, so the edit runs with only a monitoring flag.

policies.name: Document backdating interdiction
policies.description: Backdating an agreement or signature to misstate when rights arose is falsification, with potential criminal exposure for the signatories. Requests to retroactively date documents are refused outright; genuine retrospective effect is documented openly with an as-of clause by counsel.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — retrospective effect is documented via effective-date clauses, never by backdating execution

policies.name: Execution formalities for special instruments
policies.description: Deeds, powers of attorney and sealed instruments fall outside ordinary e-signature validity in Singapore — the Electronic Transactions Act excludes them — so a routing to wet-ink or witnessed execution must be confirmed by a human before the signature ceremony is set up.
regulatory_references: Singapore Electronic Transactions Act 2010 — First Schedule excluded instruments (powers of attorney, indentures)

policies.name: Automated attestation and certification interdiction
policies.description: Statements of the form 'I certify that' or 'I attest that' are personal accountability acts under regimes like MAS IAC and the Australian FAR — they cannot be generated and released by a machine on a person's behalf. Automation of certification language is hard-blocked and tenants cannot disable this rule.
regulatory_references: MAS Guidelines on Individual Accountability and Conduct (2020)
Australia Financial Accountability Regime Act 2023 — accountability obligations of accountable persons

message_contains_any: sign the attestation on my behalf

policies.name: Regulatory filing and return preparation
policies.description: Machine-prepared prudential returns and regulatory filings are acceptable as drafts, but figures and disclosures must be checked by the responsible officer before they harden into the submission pack — errors discovered post-lodgement become reportable events themselves.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — maker-checker on prudential and statutory returns

policies.name: Final lodgement with a regulator portal
policies.description: The act of lodging — pressing submit on a regulator portal — is the moment the firm goes on the record and the accountable person's name attaches to the content. A human expert performs the final lodgement even where drafting was automated.
regulatory_references: MAS Guidelines on Individual Accountability and Conduct (2020) — senior manager responsibility for material functions

policies.name: Board minutes and resolutions drafting
policies.description: Minutes are the statutory record of board proceedings under the Companies Act and are presumed evidence of what was decided. AI-drafted minutes or resolutions are reviewed by the company secretary before they enter the minute book.
regulatory_references: Singapore Companies Act 1967 s.188 — minutes of proceedings of meetings

policies.name: Statutory filing deadline reminders
policies.description: Generating reminders for annual returns, AGM notices and lodgement due dates is low-risk calendar hygiene; the flag keeps the compliance calendar owner aware of which reminders were machine-generated.

policies.name: Automated conflicts-of-interest screening
policies.description: Running a conflicts check against the matter and counterparty register is deterministic database work the engine can do unaided; results that actually reveal a conflict are handled by downstream policies, so the screening run itself is allowed with a flag.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — conflicts screening before instruction or engagement

policies.name: Investigation interview scheduling
policies.description: Sequencing who is interviewed first in an internal investigation is strategy, not calendaring — the order can tip off subjects or contaminate accounts. The investigations office approves the schedule the assistant proposes before invitations go out.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — interview sequencing controlled by the investigation lead

policies.name: Investigation interview records and summaries
policies.description: Interview notes and witness statements may become disclosable evidence or lose privilege if circulated carelessly, and AI summarisation can flatten contested nuances. A reviewer signs off any machine-generated summary before it joins the investigation file.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — verbatim records preserved alongside any summary

policies.name: Whistleblower identity protection
policies.description: Disclosing who made a protected disclosure is a criminal offence for APRA-regulated entities under the Corporations Act whistleblower regime and destroys reporting-channel trust everywhere else. Any request touching reporter identity goes straight to a human in the investigations office.
regulatory_references: Australia Corporations Act 2001 Pt 9.4AAA s.1317AAE — confidentiality of whistleblower identity

policies.name: Evidence handling and chain of custody
policies.description: Electronic evidence only survives challenge if collection, imaging and transfer are logged end to end — Singapore courts test authenticity of electronic records, and a custody gap is the standard line of attack. Each handling step the assistant prepares is countersigned by a human custodian.
regulatory_references: Singapore Evidence Act 1893 s.116A — presumptions relating to electronic records
